subsystem: MongoDB server authorization subsystem: RoleName::parseFromBSON, the deserializer that turns an untrusted BSON element (a role identity, carrying a role name and its database) into an in-memory RoleName object. It is part of the auth/role-management code path (AuthorizationManager field-name constants, RoleName type).
provenance: Identified from the symbol names and API surface: RoleName, BSONElement, embeddedObjectUserCheck(), getFields(), uassert(ErrorCodes::BadValue, ...), str::stream(), valueStringData(), and AuthorizationManager::ROLE_NAME_FIELD_NAME / ROLE_DB_FIELD_NAME are all MongoDB server (mongo::) internal APIs. The banner comment '/* sample: primevul_train_3047_vuln */' and file name train_0298_cwe20.cpp mark it as a MongoDB C++ source excerpt tagged to CWE-20 (improper input validation).
trust_boundary: The BSONElement 'elem' is attacker-controllable: BSON reaches this routine from the wire (a client command / serialized role document) or from stored documents, both of which are untrusted. Every field type, presence, and value inside the embedded object is chosen by the producer. The parser must therefore validate the type of EACH field it reads before interpreting its bytes; the hostile inputs are the two sub-fields named by ROLE_NAME_FIELD_NAME and ROLE_DB_FIELD_NAME, either of which may be absent or of the wrong BSON type.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Definition of the static/member factory RoleName::parseFromBSON. It takes one parameter, a const reference to a BSONElement, and returns a fully-constructed RoleName value. This is the deserialization entry point: it converts a piece of a BSON document (which encodes a role's identity as an embedded object with a name and a db) into the strongly-typed RoleName used elsewhere in the authorization code. Because the input is BSON, it is untrusted wire/stored data and this function is the validation gate for it.

Calls embeddedObjectUserCheck() on the incoming element to obtain the embedded BSON object. The 'UserCheck' variant throws a user-facing uassert-style error (rather than a fatal invariant) if elem is not actually of type Object, so this line enforces that the top-level element is an embedded document before any field access. After this, 'obj' is a BSONObj view into the same underlying, attacker-controlled BSON buffer.

Declares a fixed 2-element array of BSONElement to receive the two fields the parser cares about. BSONElement default-constructs to an EOO (end-of-object / 'empty') element, meaning 'field not found'. This array is the output buffer handed to getFields below; slot 0 will hold the name field, slot 1 the db field.

getFields performs a single scan of the embedded object, looking for the two named keys (the role-name field name and the role-db field name, both compile-time string constants from AuthorizationManager) and writes the matching BSONElements into 'fields' by position: fields[0] for ROLE_NAME_FIELD_NAME, fields[1] for ROLE_DB_FIELD_NAME. If a requested key is absent, the corresponding slot is left as the default EOO element. getFields does NOT validate types — it only matches keys — so after this call each slot is either a real element of an arbitrary BSON type or an EOO placeholder.

Binds nameField to slot 0 (the role-name element) and validates it. The uassert(cond) macro throws a BadValue user error if its final boolean argument is false. Here the guard is nameField.type() == String, so if the name field is missing (EOO) or is any non-String BSON type, parsing is rejected with a descriptive message. This is exactly the correct pattern: prove the element is a String before its bytes are interpreted as a string. The str::stream() builds the error message lazily.

Binds dbField to slot 1 (the role-db element) and is intended to validate that IT is a String before use. The error message correctly refers to ROLE_DB_FIELD_NAME, but the boolean guard re-checks nameField.type() == String — the wrong variable. nameField was already validated on line 12, so this second uassert is a no-op with respect to dbField: it can never reject a malformed db field. dbField's type is thereby never checked.

Constructs and returns the RoleName from the two fields' string values. valueStringData() returns a StringData view over the element's value bytes, computed for a BSON String as (pointer to value data, embedded 32-bit length prefix minus 1). nameField.valueStringData() is safe because line 12 proved nameField is a String. dbField.valueStringData() is called with NO preceding type check (line 18's guard tested nameField), so it interprets whatever bytes dbField points at as a length-prefixed string regardless of the element's real BSON type.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the core defect: a copy-paste error. The block reads dbField and is meant to enforce dbField.type() == String, but the guard tests nameField.type() == String instead. Because nameField was already asserted to be a String on line 12, this assertion is always true and never inspects dbField at all. The invariant the code MEANT to enforce (the db sub-field is a String before its bytes are read as a string) is silently unenforced. An attacker who submits a role object whose db field is present but of any non-String type — e.g. {role: "admin", db: 12345} or {role: "admin", db: {}} — sails past this gate. This is CWE-20 (improper input validation): the parser fails to validate the type of a field it subsequently trusts.

The unvalidated dbField reaches dbField.valueStringData(). For a real BSON String, valueStringData reads the 4-byte little-endian length prefix stored just before the character data and returns a StringData of (data, len-1). When dbField is actually a different type (Int/Long/Object/Array/Bool/etc.) or an EOO placeholder, the four bytes at the value position are NOT a valid string length: they are, for example, the raw integer value or the object's total-size header. valueStringData will treat those attacker-influenced bytes as the length and the following bytes as characters, producing a StringData whose pointer+length can run off the end of the element's value and past the BSON buffer. Downstream copying/reading of that StringData is then an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125): information disclosure of adjacent heap memory, or a crash (DoS). If the db field is entirely absent, dbField is EOO and valueStringData() is called on an element with no value at all, which is at best undefined behavior and at worst a NULL/invalid read (CWE-476). The safe implementation must confirm dbField.type() == String before calling valueStringData().

getFields only key-matches; it never type-checks and it tolerates missing keys by leaving EOO placeholders. The security of everything downstream therefore rests entirely on the two uassert gates that follow. Since the second gate (lines 15-18) is broken, the contract that 'every slot is a validated String before valueStringData()' is not upheld for slot 1. This is not a bug in getFields itself but a reminder that its output is raw and must be individually validated per slot — the code validates slot 0 correctly and slot 1 not at all.

summary: A copy-paste validation bug: RoleName::parseFromBSON validates the role-name field twice and never validates the role-db field's BSON type, then calls dbField.valueStringData() on it. An attacker-supplied role BSON whose db field is missing or non-String reaches valueStringData(), which reinterprets arbitrary value bytes as a length-prefixed string, yielding an out-of-bounds read (info disclosure / crash). This is a clear CWE-20 improper-input-validation flaw with a memory-safety (CWE-125) consequence.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry-point signature and trust boundary; no defect in the declaration itself, but establishes that 'elem' is untrusted BSON.
Correctly enforces that elem is an embedded object and throws a user error otherwise; this gate is sound.

Fixed 2-element output buffer sized exactly to the two requested keys; getFields writes by position, so no over-index. Default EOO elements are the 'missing field' signal, handled (or not) by the later type checks.
Key-only lookup that never validates type; correct as a primitive, but shifts all validation responsibility onto the following uassert gates.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: uassert(... nameField.type() == String) for the name field (lines 8-12)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Correctly validates fields[0] is a String before nameField.valueStringData() is used; this gate is right.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: uassert(... nameField.type() == String) for the db field (lines 14-18)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Copy-paste error: message references the db field but the guard re-tests nameField, so dbField's type is never validated (primary finding).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: dbField.valueStringData() in the RoleName constructor call (line 20)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Reads value bytes as a length-prefixed string with no preceding type check; concrete out-of-bounds-read / undefined-behavior consequence of the missing validation.

findings.title: Role db field type never validated (copy-paste: checks nameField twice)
findings.anchor: uassert(...) guard for dbField uses nameField.type() == String
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation (wrong variable checked; missing type check on a field before use)
findings.invariant: Before dbField.valueStringData() is called, the implementation must prove dbField.type() == String; the guard must test dbField, not nameField.
findings.failure_mode: The second uassert tests nameField.type() (already true from line 12) instead of dbField.type(), so a role BSON whose db field is missing or of any non-String type (int, object, array, bool, ...) passes validation. dbField then flows unchecked into valueStringData() at line 20. Impact: type confusion and downstream memory misinterpretation of an attacker-controlled field.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the guard should read dbField.type() == String (compare to the correct nameField check on line 12), and that no earlier caller re-validates dbField's type before parseFromBSON returns.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read: valueStringData() on an unvalidated (possibly non-String/EOO) element
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via type confusion (length prefix reinterpreted from non-string bytes)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0298_cwe20.cpp line 20; BSONElement::valueStringData implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: valueStringData() must only be invoked on a BSONElement whose type() == String, so its embedded 4-byte length prefix and character region are well-formed and lie within the element's value bounds.
findings.failure_mode: Because line 18 never checks dbField, an attacker sends {role:"admin", db:<non-string>} (e.g. an integer, sub-object, or omits db entirely -> EOO). valueStringData() treats the value's leading bytes as a 32-bit string length and returns StringData(data, len-1). For a non-String element those bytes are not a valid length, so the resulting view can point past the element/BSON buffer; reading or copying it discloses adjacent heap memory or crashes the server (DoS). An EOO element has no value bytes, giving a NULL/invalid read (CWE-476).
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify what BSONElement::valueStringData() does for a non-String/EOO element (whether it asserts type or blindly reads valuestrsize()/valuestr()), and that the fixed dbField type gate closes this path.

audit_checklist: Change the line 15-18 guard from nameField.type() == String to dbField.type() == String so the role-db field is actually validated.
Confirm BSONElement::valueStringData() does not itself assert type()==String; if it does, the impact is a controlled uassert rather than OOB read, but the input-validation bug remains.
Check every other parseFromBSON in the auth code (UserName, etc.) for the same duplicated-variable copy-paste pattern.
Add a unit/fuzz test with db field of type Int, Object, Array, Bool, and absent, asserting BadValue is thrown rather than a StringData being built.
Confirm getFields leaves EOO for missing keys and that both slots are type-checked before valueStringData() is ever called.
